coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots using cysteine and maltose as ecofriendly ligands for the detection of venlafaxine anti-depression drug in pharmaceutical and plasma samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sadhu, Vibhuti Atulbhai
Park, Tae Jung
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the green emissive carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated using cysteine and maltose as bio-precursors via refluxing process. The emission and excitation peaks of fabricated cysteine-maltose CDs (CM-CDs) are noticed at 511 and 420 nm, respectively. The as-fabricated CM-CDs displayed average mean size of 3.63 ± 1.12 nm with uniform spherical shape and good quantum yield of 32.81 %. Importantly, the as-fabricated CM-CDs act as a sensor for venlafaxine an anti-depression drug sensing via a fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime of CM-CDs was 1.34 ns. The as-fabricated CM-CDs were highly sensitive towards venlafaxine, displaying good linear range from 0.1 to 180 µM with the limit of detection of 75.15 nM. The fluorescence of CM-CDs was reduced with the interaction of venlafaxine, thereby favoring to develop CM-CDs-based cellulose paper sensor for venlafaxine assay. The fabricated sensor was effectively utilized for the quantification of venlafaxine in pharmaceutical and biological samples with good recovery rates (96.01––99.65 %).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
Maltose
CM-CDs
Venlafaxine
Cellulose paper-based detection
Pharmaceutical and Biofluids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of venlafaxine in real samples
Investigation of CM-CDs interactions with various drugs
Identification of venlafaxine in real samples

Department of Science and Technology, Government of India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: VAS and SKK acknowledge the Director, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat for providing necessary facility to carry out this work. SKK greatly acknowledges the Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS) project ( 37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401 ) and the Department of Science and Technology , Government of India ( EMR/2016/002621/IPC ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption, excitation/emission (420/511 nm) spectra of CM-CDs. Inset: Digital images of CM-CDs under UV light and in daylight.
(a) CM-CDs XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
HR-TEM image of CM-CDs at (a) 5 nm, (b) average size of CM-CDs, CM-CDs + venlafaxine HR-TEM images at (c) 5 nm, and (d) 10 nm scale bar.

Emission spectra of CM-CDs treated with different drugs (a) (tolbutamide, metformin hydrochloride, 5-fluorouracil, indapamide, fluconazole, venlafaxine, and lisinopril, 1 mM) and (b) digital image of CM-CDs with aforementioned drugs captured under UV light at 365 nm.
(a) Fluorescence responses of CM-CDs after introducing venlafaxine (0.1–180 μM) and (b) digital image of CM-CDs solutions with various amounts of venlafaxine when exposed to UV light at 365 nm.
Changes in green fluorescence of CM-CDs-coated paper strip with increasing venlafaxine concentrations (0.1, 8.0, 40, 80, and 180 µM) of. The digital images were taken at 365 nm of UV light.
Schematic representation of the formation of green fluorescent CM-CDs using cysteine and maltose as precursors.
Comparison of green fluorescent CM-CDs-based fluorescence method with reported analytical approaches for the identification of venlafaxine.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [HPLC- High-performance liquid chromatography, UPLC-Ultra performance liquid chromatography, Ads-DPV −Adsorptive stripping differential pulse voltammetry, SWV-Square-wave voltammetry]

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots using cysteine and maltose as ecofriendly ligands for the detection of venlafaxine anti-depression drug in pharmaceutical and plasma samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84, Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84, Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the green emissive carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated using cysteine and maltose as bio-precursors via refluxing process. The emission and excitation peaks of fabricated cysteine-maltose CDs (CM-CDs) are noticed at 511 and 420 nm, respectively. The as-fabricated CM-CDs displayed average mean size of 3.63 ± 1.12 nm with uniform spherical shape and good quantum yield of 32.81 %. Importantly, the as-fabricated CM-CDs act as a sensor for venlafaxine an anti-depression drug sensing via a fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime of CM-CDs was 1.34 ns. The as-fabricated CM-CDs were highly sensitive towards venlafaxine, displaying good linear range from 0.1 to 180 µM with the limit of detection of 75.15 nM. The fluorescence of CM-CDs was reduced with the interaction of venlafaxine, thereby favoring to develop CM-CDs-based cellulose paper sensor for venlafaxine assay. The fabricated sensor was effectively utilized for the quantification of venlafaxine in pharmaceutical and biological samples with good recovery rates (96.01––99.65 %).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel family member of fluorescent nanomaterials that have drawn a lot of interest in various research areas including optical sensors, biosensing and imaging applications [1–3]. In 2004, Xu et al. first synthesized fluorescent zero-dimensional CDs with a size of < 10 nm while purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [4]. The CDs were formed by utilizing two approaches, including “top-down” and “bottom-up”. Several carbon compounds/materials, including activated carbon, graphite, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds, and carbon soot have been used as precursors for the synthesis of CDs using top-down approaches. Moreover, a variety of distinct reactions, such as microwave-assisted, hydrothermal, thermal pyrolysis, and reflux techniques, are used to assemble tiny carbon particles, thereby forming ultra-small fluorescent carbon nanostructures via bottom-up approaches [1,5]. Numerous excellent qualities of CDs, including their fabrication with green chemistry approaches, easy to functionalize their surfaces, negligible or no toxicity, good biocompatibility, stable fluorescence, and great photostability make them specific fluorescent materials as compared to other materials [6–10]. Importantly, CDs have been effectively used as potential candidates in cell imaging, catalysis, sensing, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices, and visual sensing [11–17]. It was observed that the solvent, ligand chemistry, pH, temperature, and precursors have played key roles in altering the fluorescence properties of CDs [18]. During CDs formation, organic compounds or natural sources are frequently used as precursors, suggesting that they acted as surface passivation agents and carbon sources, which leads to form fluorescent CDs with specific optical characteristics. The use of organic precursors enhances the usability of CDs in biological and environmental applications due to their biocompatibility and functionalization capabilities. As a result, the utilization of organic molecules as precursors offers to create fluorescent CDs, exploring them as the beginning of a new era of versatile and sustainable nanomaterials for a wide range of practical applications [19]. Various organic molecules were employed as precursors to create fluorescent CDs for the development of specific fluorescent readers to various drugs assays. For example, yellow-green fluorescent CDs were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine and glyoxal as carbon sources, which demonstrated as potential probes for successful detection of tetracycline hydrochloride through a quenching mechanism with the limit of detection (LOD) of 6.0 μM [20]. Blue-emissive CDs were produced via hydrothermal method using D-glutamic acid and p-phenylenediamine for detecting an antitumor drug gefitinib [21]. Furthermore, blue fluorescent CDs were prepared by utilizing citric acid and thiosemicarbazide as precursors via hydrothermal method and explored as a sensor for detecting nitrofurantoin and dantrolene sodium [22]. Vitamin B12 was detected using blue-emissive CDs with an LOD of 100 nM [23]. Similarly, an intense red-fluorescent CDs were fabricated by utilizing fluorine and amidogen as precursors and used as a probe for fluorescence assay of hypochlorite [24]. Based on the above remarkable properties of CDs, it is clear that CDs fluorescence can be tuned by introducing two organic molecules as precursors.

Venlafaxine belongs to a class of medications called selective norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake inhibitors. Moreover, venlafaxine is chemically known as 1-[2-(dimethylamino)-1-(4-methoxyphenyl) ethyl] cyclohexanol and widely used as an antidepressant for narcolepsy, anxiety, and migraines in adults and children [25]. The usual range of suggested daily dosages for venlafaxine is 75–375 mg. Excessive dosages may give unusual biochemical pathways in human body that results cardiovasculararrest, hypertension, hypotension, serotonin syndrome, depression, coma, and even death [26]. Assessing drug levels in biofluids like plasma, serum, and urine has become increasingly important in medical or clinical perspective toevaluate its toxicity, side effects, interactions, and therapeutic efficacy. Consequently, it is an essential requirement for the investigation of sensitive analytical method for detection of venlafaxine. As a result, venlafaxine has been determined using severalanalytical techniques, including gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [27], voltammetry [28], electrochemical [29], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [30]. These techniques exhibit several disadvantages such as expensive instrumentations, time-consuming, and requiring sample pre-treatment. Furthermore, specific ligands and surface modifications are required for venlafaxine assay using electrochemical sensors. Also, the analytical features of fluorescence spectroscopic approach is greatly improved by integrating CDs as nanoprobes for sensitive identification of wide variety target molecules with lower detection limits [2,31]. This work explores the creation of fluorescence assay for the sensing of venlafaxine via a “turn-off” mechanism using CDs prepared from cysteine and maltose as precursors. The main significance of the proposed work is to utilize biocompatible and non-toxic bio-ligands for the formation of fluorescent CDs through a simple synthetic procedure.

Herein, a simple one-step refluxing process was used to create green fluorescent CDs using cysteine and maltose (CM-CDs) as precursors. Cysteine is a mercapto group containing aminoacid and maltose is a disaccharide, which favors to generate CM-CDs with specific fluorescence characteristics, good quantum yield (QY) and surface functionalities. Further, both molecules are biocompatible, non-toxic, cost-effective and allowed precise control of CDs size and surface chemistry. The prepared CM-CDs exhibited green fluorescence when exposed to UV light at 365 nm wavelength (Scheme 1 ), displaying a remarkable QY of 32.81 %. The as-fabricated CM-CDs exhibited excitation/emission wavelengths of 420/511 nm. The as-fabricated CM-CDs acted as a remarkable fluorescent probe for venlafaxine assay via “turn-off” phenomenon. The created CM-CDs fluorescent sensor displayed the LOD of 75.15 nM with a good linear response of 0.1–180 µM for venlafaxine. Interestingly, the potentiality of CM-CDs as a fluorescent probe was demonstrated for sensing of venlafaxine on surface modified paper with CM-CDs. Additionally, the developed CM-CDs-based fluorescent probe was successfully demonstrated for sensing of venlafaxine in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples.

Cysteine and maltose were purchased from SRL Chemicals, India. Tolbutamide, metformin hydrochloride, 5-fluorouracil, indapamide, fluconazole, venlafaxine, and lisinopril were received as gift samples from Cadila Pharma. Velnor-XR 75 capsule of Cipla Ltd. was procured from a medical store, Surat, India. Analytical-grade chemicals were utilized throughout the entire analysis, and Milli-Q filtered water was used for the experiments.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, USA) was utilized to study excitation and emission characteristics of CM-CDs. Surface charge and hydrodynamic size were determined by using zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) of SZ-100 Horiba, respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEM 2100 device from JEOL, Japan) was utilized to analyze the morphological structures of CM-CDs. The absorption spectra of CM-CDs were determined through a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocen Optics, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Alpha II Bruker, Germany) was employed to confirm the surface functionality of CM-CDs.

Here, one-step reaction was used to generate green fluorescent CM-CDs using cysteine and maltose as reagents. Briefly, 60 mM of cysteine and 5 mM of maltose were dissolved in water and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. The prepared solutions of cysteine (5 mL) and maltose (5 mL) were refluxed for 12 h at 160 °C. During the process, the pH of the reaction mixture was adjusted to 9 by adding 100 µL of 0.1 M NaOH. After completion of the reaction, the color of the solution was turned to yellow. The prepared CM-CDs solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 1.5 h, favoring to separate ultra-small CM-CDs nanostructures, which displays intense green fluorescence upon exposure to UV light at wavelength of 365 nm. The resultant solution of CM-CDs was stored at 4 °C for further studies.

In order to examine CM-CDs as a selective probe for fluorescence assay of venlafaxine, 0.5 mL of several drugs (tolbutamide, metformin hydrochloride, 5-fluorouracil, indapamide, fluconazole, venlafaxine and lisinopril, 1.0 mM, 0.5 mL) were added separately to1 mL solution of CM-CDs. The fluorescence characteristics of CM-CDs with the above drugs were examined after vertexing sample solutions for 60 s. The fluorescence spectra of the solutions mentioned above were determined at 420 nm of excitation wavelength. Interestingly, only venlafaxine was remarkably influenced the characteristic emission peak of CM-CDs, showing 69.7 % of fluorescence quenching by venlafaxine whereas other drugs did not alter the emission spectra, which indicates that CM-CDs act as a simple fluorescent sensor for venlafaxine identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of venlafaxine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Green emissive CM-CDs were employed for the identification of venlafaxine in real samples, including tablet Velnor-XR 75 and various biofluids such as urine, blood, and serum. Biofluid samples were collected from Milan Laboratory, Surat, Gujarat, India. The Velnor-XR 75 capsule was acquired from local medical shop, Surat and crushed into a powder using a mortar and pestle. The prepared powder was dissolved in 10 mL of water, and subsequently sonicated for 5 min, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm. Three different solutions were prepared by diluting Velnor-XR 75 tablet into various concentrations of 1.25, 2.5, and 5.0 mg/mL, and the amount of venlafaxine was estimated by the standard calibration graph. Additionally, the biofluid samples including, blood, urine, and serum were diluted 10-fold using distilled water. The resulting diluted samples were then treated with various concentrations of venlafaxine (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, and 8.0 µM). To measure the amount of venlafaxine in the solutions, fluorescence spectra were acquired after treating them with as-fabricated CM-CDs, and the obtained spectral analysis was used to quantify venlafaxine drug in biological samples.

The synthesis of green fluorescent CM-CDs was performed in a single-step reaction using cysteine and maltose as reagents. During the reflexion at high temperature, cysteine and maltose are effectively utilized to build-up carbogenic core network nanostructures, which results to form CM-CDs with remarkable optical characteristics. In order to generate CM-CDs with good spectral characteristics, several parameters (composition of cysteine and maltose, reaction temperature, time and pH) were examined for the preparation of green CM-CDs. The best optimal conditions were confirmed by characterizing the formed green CM-CDs via spectroscopic and electron microscopic techniques. For example, fluorescence emission spectral characteristics of CM-CDs were investigated by refluxing the mixture of cysteine: maltose at different molar ratios (15:1.25, 30:2.5, 60:5, 120:10 mM) and the spectral data were depicted in Fig. S1a of the Supporting Information. It can be revealed that the highest emission peak intensity of CM-CDs was achieved by utilizing cysteine and maltose molar ratio of 60:5 mM, signifying molar ratio of 60:5 mM (cysteine:maltose) was the best composition to generate high photoluminescence carbogenic core network nanostructures. Additionally, the refluxing temperature and time were tuned by performing reaction at different reaction temperatures from 80 to 200 °C and at reaction times (6–24 h using cysteine:maltose molar ratio of 60:5 mM. As seen in Fig. S1b-c of the Supporting Information, the high fluorescence intensity was recorded at reaction temperature of 160 °C for 12 h of the reaction time, confirming 160 °C reaction temperature and 12 h reaction time are optimum conditions for the fabrication of CM-CDs. After completion of the reaction, the resulting solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 0.5–3.0 h, indicating that 1.5 h is the optimum dialysis time for CM-CDs with good fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1d of the Supporting Information) [32]. After optimizing reaction conditions, the emission spectra of CM-CDs were examined at distinct pH of reaction solution (Fig. S2a of the Supporting Information), demonstrating that pH 9.0 of reaction solution was found to be the best pH for the preparation of CM-CDs. Furthermore, cysteine and maltose were used as individuals precursors for the fabrication of CDs under the same reaction conditions (Fig. S2b of the Supporting Information). It can be observed that the highest emission intensity was observed by using cysteine-maltose mixture as compared to the individual use of cysteine and maltose, signifying that the fluorescence emission spectral characteristics of CDs were greatly enhanced by using cysteine-maltose mixture as a precursor. To generate an effective photoluminescence CM-CDs-based carbogenic core network nanostructures, molar ratio at 60:5 mM of cysteine:maltose, 160 °C reaction temperature, 12 h reaction time, pH 9.0 and 1.5 h dialysis time were determined to be optimum for the fabrication of CM-CDs with intense emission spectral analysis. These results indicated that the synthesis of CM-CDs using cysteine and maltose through a refluxing method promotes the formation of carbogenic core network nanostructures with active functional groups and surface passivation.

Additionally, optical (excitation/emission and absorption) characteristics of as-prepared CM-CDs were studied by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopic techniques. Emission spectra of CM-CDs were examined under various λEx ranging from 350 to 450 nm to confirm the best λEm for CM-CDs (Figure S3 of the Supporting Information). The emission spectra reveal that CM-CDs display broad emission spectra and excitation-dependent properties, resulting to exhibit λEm at 456, 478, 491, 500, 503, 504, 505, 511, 510, 509, and 509 nm when applied λEx at 350, 360, 370, 380, 390, 400, 410, 420, 430, 440, and 450 nm, respectively. The maximum emission spectra of CM-CDs were noticed λEm at 511 nm under λEx at 420 nm, and the intensity of CM-CDs was decreased either above or below excitation wavelengths. These emission spectral properties demonstrated that broad emission spectra and excitation-dependent spectral characteristics are basic phenomenon in carbogenic core network nanostructures, which also confirms that the existence of various energy levels in CM-CDs with hetero atoms (N, S, and O). Consequently, the optimal excitation wavelength for additional analytical research studies was performed by applying λEx at 420 nm. The absorption spectrum of CM-CDs was recorded, showing λmax at 314 nm (Figure S4 of the Supporting Information), which corresponds to π–π* and n–π* transitions in CM-CDs due to the existence of active surface groups. Moreover, the emission peak of CM-CDs was observed at 511 nm (λEx = 420 nm). To confirm the absorption and fluorescence characteristics of CM-CDs, the absorption and emission spectra of precursors (cysteine and maltose) and CM-CDs were examined and shown in Fig. S4a-b of the Supporting Information, revealing that maltose and cysteine displayed λmax at 274 and 237 nm, which are not noticed in the absorption spectrum of CM-CDs (λmax = 314 nm). Moreover, neither cysteine nor maltose showsany emission peaks in their fluorescence spectra. Nonetheless, after the formation of CM-CDs, there was a recognizable emission peak in the fluorescence spectrum of CM-CDs as compared to the spectra of precursors. The digital images of CM-CDs dispersion in water under irradiation of UV light (365 nm) and in daylight were shown in Inset of Fig. 1 , exhibiting intense green fluorescence and light yellow color in daylight. The fluorescence QY of CM-CDs was determined by the described method in the literature [33], showing the QY of 32.81 % where quinine sulfate was utilized as a reference. Furthermore, the photostability of the synthesized CM-CDs was assessed over a period ranging from 0.5 to 24 h. The emission spectra of the CM-CDs remain stable under UV light for up to 3 h. However, after 3 h, a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. S5a of the Supporting Information), indicating that the as-prepared CM-CDs displayed good photostability. A stability assessment was conducted to investigate the stability of the as-fabricated CM-CDs at different time intervals (1 – 150 days) (Fig. S5b of the Supporting Information), revealing that the CM-CDs remained stable up to 20 days with very little variation in the emission peak intensity, however the emission peak intensity of CM-CDs decreases to 65 %. Overall, the green fluorescent CM-CDs derived from cysteine and maltose mixture displayed good stability and remarkable optical characteristics, which makes them suitable to use as a rapid and simple sensor for sensing application with outstanding selectivity and sensitivity.

FT-IR analysis was utilized to identify the functional groups on the surface of CM-CDs derived from cysteine and maltose (Fig. S5a of the Supporting Information). The –OH stretching peaks of cysteine and maltose were noticed at 3439 and 3414 cm−1, respectively, while after the formation of CM-CDs, the –OH stretching peak was noticed at 3440 cm−1. Further, –NH stretching peak was observed in FT-IR spectrum of cysteine at 3000 cm−1 due to its amino group. Additionally, −SH and C=O groups stretching peaks displayed at 2544 cm−1 and 2094 cm−1, and –NH bending peak of cysteine was noticed at 1588 cm−1. The –CH and −C=C stretching frequencies are appeared at 2926 cm−1 and 1628 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra of maltose and CM-CDs, respectively. The C–H bending peaks of cysteine and maltose are observed at 1500 and 1414 cm−1, respectively, while after the formation of CM-CDs, the peak was noticed at 1397 cm−1. The sharp –OH bending peak was appeared at 1404 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of cysteine, however, it was not noticed in the FT-IR spectrum of the as-fabricated CM-CDs. The intensities of −C-O stretching peaks in between 1129–1035 cm−1 were slightly varied in the FT-IR spectra of precursors and CM-CDs, suggesting the formation of the successful formation of CM-CDs nanostructures [34,35].

The details of elements and their oxidation states were validated by utilizing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The CM-CDs survey spectrum indicates the binding energies for S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s at 165.98, 287.20, 400.47, 536.47, and 1070.66 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 a). In CM-CDs, the percentages of S, C, N, O, and Na are 7.65 %, 53.87 %, 6.55 %, 27.96 %, and 3.97 %, respectively. In the XPS spectrum, the binding energies of C1s at 284.80 and 287.20 eV belong to C=C and C–C/C=C/C-N, as illustrated in Fig. 2b [36]. The existence of the C-N group is verified by noticing the peak at 400.47 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2c). Additionally, the binding energy of O1s at 536.47 eV is attributed to the C=O group, as depicted in Fig. 2d. The size of CM-CDs was investigated using HR-TEM analysis. Fig. 3 a–b shows the HR-TEM images of CM-CDs, revealing the CM-CDs are in uniform dispersion with an average size of 3.63 ± 1.12 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to elucidate the structure of synthesized CM-CDs. The as-fabricated CM-CDs presented diffraction peaks at 2θ angles of 18.95° and 28.51°, corresponding to the (110) and (002) planes, respectively as depicted in Figure S6 of the Supporting Information [37]. These findings suggest that the as-fabricated CM-CDs possess a semi-crystalline structure, as evidenced by the presence of these characteristic diffraction peaks. Zeta potential data revealed that as-synthesized CM-CDs displayed a negative surface charge of −27.10 mV (Figure S7a of the Supporting Information). Additionally, DLS analysis demonstrated that the hydrodynamic diameter of CM-CDs is 3.89 nm (Figure S8a of the Supporting Information). Furthermore, fluorescence decay time data revealed that the as-fabricated CM-CDs exhibited a mean lifetime of 1.34 ns (Figure S9a of the Supporting Information). These spectral data signified that the formation of green fluorescent CM-CDs derived from cysteine and maltose as reagents, which favors to the molecular recognition applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of CM-CDs interactions with various drugs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore CM-CDs as a facile fluorescent probe, aqueous suspensions of CM-CDs were treated with a series of drugs (tolbutamide, metformin hydrochloride, 5-fluorouracil, indapamide, fluconazole, venlafaxine, and lisinopril, 1 mM) and recorded their emission spectra by applying excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The as-prepared CM-CDs emission spectra were recorded at 511 nm for all drugs and shown in Fig. 4 a. Surprisingly, a markable fluorescence emission quenching (70.73 %) of green CM-CDs was noticed in the case of venlafaxine, whereas remaining drugs did not cause any significant spectral changes and almost unchanged in the intensity as well as emission spectra of CM-CDs. Subsequently, digital images of green CM-CDs with the above drugs were taken by exposing them under 365 nm of UV light (Fig. 4b), displaying a drastic change in green fluorescence, which confirms that green CM-CDs could be used as a probe for venlafaxine assay via fluorescence turn-off phenomena.

To evaluate sensitivity of green CM-CDs as a probe for venlafaxine assay, the emission spectra of green CM-CDs were examined by progressively increasing concentration of venlafaxine from 0.1 to 180 µM (Fig. 5 a). It can be observed that the emission intensity of CM-CDs was steadily quenched with increasing the concentration of venlafaxine, favoring to create a calibration graph between the concentrations of venlafaxine and the emission intensity ratio of as-fabricated CM-CDs at 511 nm. The ratio of emission intensity at 511 nm (I0/I), where I0 and I indicate the emission intensity of CM-CDs without and with venlafaxine, respectively. As can be observed from Figure S10 of the Supporting Information, with increasing concentrations of venlafaxine (0.1 to 180 µM), a good linear correlation between the ratio of emission peak of CM-CDs at 511 nm and the concentrations of venlafaxine (0.1 – 10 µM) was described as y = 0.0456x + 1.0768 (R2 = 0.9914), where “y” is the fluorescence intensity of CM-CDs at 511 nm and “x” is the venlafaxine concentration. The LOD of the probe was found to be 75.15 nM for venlafaxine, which was assessed by using the formula 3σ/s, where “σ” stands for the standard deviation of CM-CDs and “s” for the slope of a calibration graph. Additionally, the changes in green fluorescence of CDs with various amounts of venlafaxine (0.1–180 μM) were examined by irradiating solutions under 365 nm of UV light, revealing that the green fluorescence of CM-CDs was steadily vanished as the concentration of venlafaxine increased (Fig. 5b). As shown in Table 1 , the developed method exhibited superior sensitivity for venlafaxine assay as compared to HPLC and voltammetric techniques.

The as-synthesized green fluorescent CM-CDs contain different types of active functional groups (–NH2, –OH, –COOH, −SH, and –CO), which confirmed from the FT-IR, and XPS spectral data. The interaction of venlafaxine drug with the surface active functional groups would result a remarkable changes in fluorescence properties, size and morphology of CM-CDs. To illustrate sensing mechanism, several analytical techniques including zeta potential, fluorescence lifetime, DLS, UV–visible, FT-IR, XPS, HR-TEM, and fluorescence spectra of CM-CDs were investigated in the existence of venlafaxine. Fig. S4a-b of the Supporting Information displays the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CM-CDs, venlafaxine, and CM-CDs + venlafaxine. The absorption spectra revealed that venlafaxine showed the maximum absorbance at 312 nm, however the intensity of absorption spectrum is greatly increased after its addition into CM-CDs. Additionally, the emission spectra of fabricated CM-CDs displayed a noticeable decrease in the emission intensity of CM-CDs with venlafaxine, whereas venlafaxine did not exhibit any peak, suggesting the addition of venlafaxine causes observable changes in the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CM-CDs. Fig. S5b of the Supporting Information displays the FT-IR spectra of CM-CDs, venlafaxine, and CM-CDs + venlafaxine. The FT-IR spectrum of venlafaxine exhibits distinct functional groups. The peak at 3405 cm−1 indicates –OH stretching, while peaks between 2928–2667 cm−1 correlated to C–H stretching. Additionally, aromatic C=H stretching and C-O stretching are observed at 1615 cm−1 and 1512 cm−1, respectively. Venlafaxine also exhibits C–H bending, C-N stretching, and C-O-C stretching groups, characterized by absorption peaks at 1456, 1250, and 1036 cm−1, respectively. After being treated with venlafaxine, the intensities of characteristic functional groups of CM-CDs were decreased and the –NH/OH stretching peak appeared at 3443 cm−1 was slightly decreased in the FT-IR spectrum of CM-CDs-venlafaxine. Also, the changes were observed in C=C stretching peak at 1633 cm−1, C–H bending at 1399 cm−1, and C-O stretching at 1035 cm−1. These spectral characteristics signify that the interaction of venlafaxine with surface functional groups of CM-CDs. When venlafaxine was introduced into CM-CDs, the well dispersed CM-CDs were aggregated, resulting to from CM-CDs-venlafaxine nanoaggregates, which leads to increase the size of CM-CDs (Fig. 3c-d). Moreover, the CM-CDs zeta potential was measured at −27.10 mV. Upon the addition of venlafaxine, the surface charge decreased to −9.62 mV, confirming the electrostatic interaction between CM-CDs and venlafaxine (Figure S7b of the Supporting Information). Furthermore, venlafaxine has aromatic ring and active functional groups including –OH, –NH2, and −C-O that facilitates to generate non-covalent interaction (π-π interactions and H-bonding) between the CM-CDs and venlafaxine, resulting the formation of CM-CDs-venlafaxine nanoaggregates, which leads to cause the quenching of fluorescence spectra. The introduction of venlafaxine into CM-CDs causes to increase the hydrodynamic size from 3.89 to 8.10 nm, as displayed in Figure S8b of the Supporting Information. The fluorescence lifetime data indicate that a slight increase in the decay time of CM-CDs from 1.34 to 1.37 ns upon the addition of venlafaxine, (Figure S9a of the Supporting Information). This confirms that the emission intensity of CM-CDs was quenched after treatment with venlafaxine due to the static quenching mechanism. Moreover, the absorption spectrum of venlafaxine is not overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CM-CDs (Figure S9d of the Supporting Information), indicating that the inner filter effect (IFE) is not involved in sensing of venlafaxine. The developed probe could be used for the detection of venlafaxine.

The selectivity of the as-prepared CM-CDs for venlafaxine assay was performed in aqueous solution by examining the emission spectra of CM-CDs upon λEx at 420. In this study, several interfering chemical substances were used in the selectivity, including drugs (imipramine hydrochloride, ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin, 1 mM), metal ions (Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, and Co2+, 1 mM), and anions (Cl-, NO3 – and SO4 2-, 1 mM) and the data were depicted in Figure S11a-b of the Supporting Information [49–51]. As is obviously evident, the emission spectra of CM-CDs were significantly quenched by venlafaxine even in the existence of interfering drugs, metal ions and anions, whereas interfering drugs, metal ions and anions showed negligible quenching impact on the CM-CDs fluorescence spectra. All of the above emission spectral data clearly illustrated that the as-prepared green fluorescent CM-CDs exhibited specific selectivity toward venlafaxine, indicating they could be used as a promising probe for selective identification of venlafaxine in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

Due to the outstanding advantages of green fluorescent CM-CDs, the potentiality of CM-CDs as a probe was demonstrated by solid-state fluorescence identification of venlafaxine, which explores that CM-CDs could be used to develop fluorescent strip for fluorescence turn-off assay of venlafaxine. In this, cellulose paper was initially cut into 1 cm diameter pieces and immersed in a 1 mL solution of CM-CDs. The CM-CDs coated paper strips were then left to dry at room temperature. Subsequently, various concentrations of venlafaxine (0.1, 8.0, 40, 80, and 180 µM) were applied onto CM-CDs modified paper strips. After drying the CM-CDs coated cellulose paper strips, the paper strips were exposed to UV light at 365 nm, leading to a visual fluorescence color change (Fig. 6 ). As can be clearly seen, the green fluorescence gradually diminished with increasing venlafaxine concentration. Thus, the CM-CDs coated cellulose paper could be utilized as a sensor for visual fluorescence sensing of venlafaxine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of venlafaxine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical usefulness of CM-CDs in quantifying venlafaxine in pharmaceutical and biofluids samples, distinct concentrations (1.25, 2.5, and 5 mg/mL) were prepared from Velnor-XR 75 tablet and quantified venlafaxine concentration by the developed method. Subsequently, various concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, and 8.0 µM) of venlafaxine were spiked with various biofluids (blood, serum, and urine) and the emission spectra were studied instantaneously to estimate the recovery of venlafaxine. The analytical results for venlafaxine assays in Velnor-XR 75 tablet were nearly identical to the real values, as revealed in Table S1 of the Supporting Information. Furthermore, the recoveries of venlafaxine from biofluids (blood, serum, and urine) lie in the range of 96.01 to 99.65 % with acceptable relative standard deviation as represented in Table S2 of the Supporting Information. These results demonstrate that the superior accuracy and precision of CM-CDs for venlafaxine is examined in pharmaceutical and biological specimens.

In summary, cysteine and maltose mixture was successfully converted into the formation of green fluorescent carbogenic core network nanostructures (CM-CDs) via simple reflexing process. The formed CM-CDs exhibited good dispersion in water with good QY of 32.81 %, showing λEm/Ex at 511/420 nm. A remarkable fluorescence quenching (69.7 %) of green fluorescent CM-CDs was noticed with the addition of venlafaxine drug, displaying a wider linearity (0.1–180 µM) with an impressive LOD of 75.15 nM for venlafaxine. Furthermore, CM-CDs were coated on cellulose paper, thereby facilitating to develop solid-state paper-strip for fluorescence detection of venlafaxine, which allows to visualize fluorescence color changes with naked-eye. Importantly, CM-CDs displayed good potentiality for assaying venlafaxine in Venlor-XR75 tablet and in biofluids (blood, serum, and urine) with impressive percentage recoveries. Thus, the as-fabricated CM-CDs offer a new advancement in establishing cost-effective miniaturized fluorescence analytical method for sensing of venlafaxine in pharmaceutical samples and biofluids.
Vibhuti Atulbhai Sadhu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tae Jung Park: . Suresh Kumar Kailasa: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VAS and SKK acknowledge the Director, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat for providing necessary facility to carry out this work. SKK greatly acknowledges the Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS) project (37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401) and the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.112980.

ce:section.ce:para:
